metadata.summary: Implement AWS security foundations: IAM Identity Center federation, IRSA for EKS, KMS encryption, Secrets Manager, GuardDuty, Security Hub, and network controls via Terraform.

content.title: AWS IAM and Security Foundations
content.summary: AWS security foundations span identity (IAM Identity Center, IRSA, least privilege), encryption (KMS at rest, TLS in transit, Secrets Manager), network controls (security groups, NACLs, PrivateLink, WAF), and detection (GuardDuty, Security Hub, Config). All layers must be active in production — each addresses a distinct failure mode that the others cannot compensate for.

why.p: IAM misconfiguration is the leading cause of AWS security incidents. Overly broad policies, long-lived access keys, and missing MFA are exploited routinely. IAM Identity Center as the single human entry point eliminates long-lived credentials; IRSA for EKS pod workloads eliminates instance-profile over-sharing; customer-managed KMS keys give auditable encryption at rest with rotation. These three layers together address the most common IAM attack vectors.

Detection (GuardDuty + Security Hub) catches anomalies that preventive controls miss: credential exfiltration, EC2 metadata service abuse, and cross-account unusual API calls. Without detection, a compromised role can operate unnoticed for weeks. Security Hub aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, and Config into one dashboard scored against CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark and AWS Foundational Security Best Practices.

list.item: Designing the IAM layer for any new workload or environment baseline.
Auditing an existing account for credential exposure, wildcard policies, or missing encryption.
Setting up EKS clusters where pods need AWS service access (S3, Secrets Manager, DynamoDB).
Compliance reviews that require encryption at rest and in transit evidence (KMS key IDs, ACM cert ARNs).
Day-2 incident response — use incident response runbooks; this methodology is for design and baseline setup.
Non-AWS clouds — GCP uses Workload Identity Federation; use gcp-security-iam instead.

section.name: identity-access
section.p: Identity and access management rules for human users and workload identities.

rule.statement: IAM Identity Center MUST be the only human entry point to AWS accounts. Long-lived IAM user access keys MUST NOT exist for human users. Root accounts MUST have MFA enabled and root access management enabled for all member accounts. All human sessions use temporary credentials via IAM Identity Center role assumption with a maximum session duration of 1–8 hours depending on the permission set sensitivity.
rule.rationale: Long-lived access keys are the primary vector for credential compromise — they don't expire, they get checked into git, they are copied to laptops. IAM Identity Center sessions are time-bounded and federated from the IdP (Okta, Entra ID, Google Workspace), so revoking an employee in the IdP immediately revokes AWS access. Root account MFA prevents org-level takeover.

rule.statement: EKS pod workloads MUST use IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) — an OIDC provider configured for the cluster, with service account annotations pointing to a scoped IAM role. Instance profiles MUST NOT be used for pod-level AWS access. IAM policies attached to IRSA roles MUST scope Resource to specific ARNs — no Resource: "*" in production IRSA policies.
rule.rationale: Instance profiles grant all pods on a node the same permissions, violating least privilege. IRSA binds a specific IAM role to a specific Kubernetes service account in a specific namespace; a compromised pod cannot steal another pod's credentials. Scoped resources prevent lateral movement within the account if one pod is compromised.

section.name: irsa-terraform
section.p: Terraform example for IRSA role with scoped S3, Secrets Manager, and KMS permissions.

example.title: IRSA role with scoped S3 and Secrets Manager access
example.note: The OIDC condition binds this role to exactly one namespace:service-account pair. A pod in a different namespace cannot assume this role.

section.name: encryption
section.p: Encryption must cover all three states: at rest (KMS), in transit (TLS), and secrets (Secrets Manager with rotation).

rule.statement: All storage at rest MUST be encrypted: S3 default encryption enabled, EBS encryption by default enabled at the account level, RDS and Aurora storage encryption enabled at cluster creation. Customer-managed KMS keys (CMKs) MUST be used for all sensitive data — do not use AWS-managed keys for data classified as confidential or restricted. Key rotation MUST be enabled on all CMKs.
rule.rationale: AWS-managed keys are shared across accounts in the same region; customer-managed keys give exclusive control and a per-key audit trail in CloudTrail. Key rotation reduces the blast radius of a key compromise. EBS default encryption ensures no developer accidentally creates an unencrypted volume.

rule.statement: All credentials and sensitive configuration MUST be stored in Secrets Manager with automatic rotation configured. No secrets in environment variables, no secrets in code, no secrets in Parameter Store standard tier (which has no encryption at rest). ALBs MUST enforce TLS 1.2 as the minimum protocol. Database connections from applications MUST use TLS.
rule.rationale: Environment variables are visible in ECS task definitions, CloudFormation templates, and container inspection commands. Secrets Manager encrypts at rest with KMS, audits every access in CloudTrail, and automates rotation for RDS, Redshift, and Elasticsearch without downtime.

example.title: Customer-managed KMS key with CloudWatch Logs access

section.name: detection
section.p: GuardDuty and Security Hub provide threat detection and posture management across the organization.

example.title: GuardDuty and Security Hub org-level setup

section.name: network-security
section.p: Network security controls: security groups, NACLs, PrivateLink, WAF, and TLS enforcement.

list.item: Security groups — instance-level stateful firewall. Use least-privilege inbound rules scoped to specific source security group IDs, not CIDR blocks, where possible. Lock down the default security group in every VPC (no inbound, no outbound).
NACLs — subnet-level stateless protection. Use as a second layer for subnet-level blocking (e.g., deny specific external CIDRs).
VPC Flow Logs — capture all traffic for security auditing and incident investigation. Write to S3 in the log-archive account.
PrivateLink — expose services privately between VPCs without peering or Transit Gateway for specific service-to-service flows.
WAF — Application-layer protection for public ALBs and CloudFront distributions. Enable AWS managed rule groups (Core, Known Bad Inputs, SQL Injection) plus rate-based rules.

checklist.check: IAM Identity Center configured as primary access — no long-lived access keys for humans.
External IdP integration (Okta, Entra ID, or Google Workspace) if applicable.
Permission sets defined for common roles (ReadOnly, PowerUser, Admin).
MFA required for all users; root account MFA enabled.
Root access disabled for all member accounts.
Custom IAM policies use least privilege — no wildcard Resource ARNs in production.
Service-linked roles used where available (no custom trust policies for AWS services that provide them).
OIDC provider configured for every EKS cluster.
IRSA service accounts annotated with specific IAM role ARNs — no instance profile pod access.
S3 default encryption enabled on all buckets.
EBS encryption by default enabled at the account level.
RDS encryption enabled at cluster creation.
Customer-managed KMS keys for confidential/restricted data.
Key rotation enabled on all CMKs.
TLS 1.2 minimum enforced on all ALBs and CloudFront distributions.
ACM certificates for all public endpoints (no self-signed in prod).
Secrets Manager for all credentials; automatic rotation configured.
No secrets in environment variables, code, or Parameter Store standard tier.
GuardDuty enabled organization-wide with S3, EKS audit logs, and malware protection.
Security Hub enabled with CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark and AWS Foundational Security Best Practices standards.
WAF protecting all public ALBs and CloudFront distributions.

section.name: iam-prompts
section.prompt:
Design IAM strategy for [USE_CASE]:
Context: - Multi-account: [YES/NO] - Identity source: [IAM_IDENTITY_CENTER/EXTERNAL_IDP/IAM_USERS] - Workloads: [HUMAN/SERVICE/BOTH] - EKS clusters: [NUMBER] (for IRSA)
Requirements: - Role types needed: [LIST] - Permission boundaries: [YES/NO] - Cross-account access: [YES/NO] - Temporary credentials only: [YES/NO]
Deliverables: 1. Permission set design (IAM Identity Center) 2. Role naming convention 3. Policy templates (S3, Secrets, DynamoDB, etc.) 4. IRSA configuration for EKS 5. Cross-account trust patterns 6. Audit and compliance controls

Create least privilege IAM policy:
Service account: [NAME] Purpose: [DESCRIPTION]
Required access: - S3: [BUCKET_ARNS] [ACTIONS] - Secrets Manager: [PREFIX_PATTERN] - DynamoDB: [TABLE_ARNS] [ACTIONS] - SQS: [QUEUE_ARNS] [ACTIONS] - KMS: [KEY_ARNS] - Other: [SPECIFY]
Constraints: - Condition keys: [IP/VPC/MFA/TIME] - Resource constraints: Required
Output: JSON policy with comments explaining each statement

Review AWS security posture for [ACCOUNT/ORGANIZATION]:
Scope: - Accounts: [LIST] or organization-wide - Focus areas: [IAM/NETWORK/DATA/COMPLIANCE/ALL] - Compliance framework: [SPECIFY]
Check: 1. IAM — MFA enforcement, access key rotation, permission boundaries, least privilege adherence 2. Network — VPC design, security group rules, public exposure, flow log analysis 3. Data Protection — encryption at rest, encryption in transit, key management, data classification 4. Detection — GuardDuty findings, Security Hub score, Config compliance, CloudTrail coverage
Output: Risk matrix, remediation priorities, terraform code for fixes

prompt.purpose: Quick IAM role for EKS IRSA

section.prompt: Create IAM role for [SERVICE_ACCOUNT] in EKS: - Cluster: [NAME] - Namespace: [NS] - S3 access: [BUCKETS] - Secrets access: [PREFIX] - DynamoDB: [TABLES]
